mechanism: Participants execute a task-switching paradigm where they view stimuli and produce responses conditional on explicit cues. Two response sets are defined (one dimension vs another), and switching involves reselecting the active response set in each trial. The reaction time difference within mixed blocks (local switch cost) and the global cost of maintaining two active sets versus one (mixing effect) are compared between groups. Analysis partitions reaction time and accuracy into cost components via factorial repeated-measures design.

Participants rate the subjective rarity of events and estimate the length of the sequence from which each event was drawn. A comparison is made between the perceived sequence length for rare versus common events with identical statistical probability. The effect is measured as the difference in estimated sequence length as a function of rated rarity, controlling for actual rarity. Linear or logistic regression quantifies the relationship between rarity perception and sequence-length judgment.

Participants select among multi-attribute choice alternatives and their choices are recorded. The effect of regret salience (experimental manipulation of cognitive accessibility) on choice patterns is measured via logit or linear regression of choice on decoy presence, treatment condition, and their interaction. Group-level proportions of decoy-driven choices are compared across conditions. Mediation analysis examines whether justification-standard activation predicts the treatment effect.

Participants read a scenario describing an event and receive either a denial of false information or an alternative causal explanation. Subsequent beliefs or attitudes regarding the event are measured on a scale. The effect of explanation type on belief change is estimated via between-subjects comparison using t-test or ANOVA. Interaction with evidence strength is tested via factorial analysis.

Entities complete a knowledge test in two conditions: one with gender-stereotype activation and one without. Test scores are compared between groups and genders via factorial ANOVA. The stereotype-threat effect is operationalized as the interaction between treatment condition and gender. Mediation by self-reported anxiety or cognitive load may also be estimated.
domain: stereotype threat and gender differences in political knowledge

mechanism: Participants are primed with trait concepts (communion or agency dimension) via a cognitive task. Temperature perception is then measured via self-report or physiological assessment. Linear regression estimates the effect of prime condition on temperature perception, with separate analysis for positive vs negative attributes and for morality vs sociability subdimensions. Between-subjects or within-subjects comparisons quantify the effect size.

Participants are classified into four groups based on diagnostic criteria (ASPD+psychopathy, ASPD-only, neither, control). Criminal activity (counts) and emotion-processing variables are recorded. Group means are compared via one-way ANOVA or Kruskal-Wallis test. Pairwise contrasts examine whether psychopathy modulates ASPD outcomes. Correlation analysis quantifies relationships between emotion dysfunction and criminal severity.

Participants making decisions about continued investment in a failing project are exposed to one of several intervention strategies: outcome-threat reduction, minimum-target setting, or process-based evaluation. Subsequent continuation decisions or resource reallocation are measured. Between-subjects comparisons via ANOVA or chi-square test quantify deescalation effectiveness. Interaction effects with project characteristics are analyzed.
domain: organizational decision-making and escalation of commitment

mechanism: Entities are assessed on Big Five personality dimensions (continuous scales). Leadership behavior is measured via multi-item scales or observer ratings. Linear regression or structural equation modeling predicts leadership outcomes from personality factors. Main effects of individual traits and controlled interactions are estimated. Multiple samples and multiple personality instruments are used to establish generalization.

Participants recall past regretted decisions and categorize them as action or inaction. Temporal distance (time since event) is recorded. Regret intensity is measured via scale or ordinal ranking. A cross-tabulation or logit model quantifies the relationship between action/inaction status and regret intensity, stratified by temporal distance. The mechanism tests whether the odds of action-regret are higher in short-term versus long-term periods.

Mortality salience is induced via a manipulation, and accessibility of death-related thoughts is measured via word-completion or lexical-decision task latencies. Subsequent prejudice or intergroup-bias measures are recorded. Mediation analysis tests whether accessibility of death-related thoughts predicts bias, conditional on whether attention is directed toward or away from mortality. Regression quantifies the direct and indirect effects.

Individuals are classified into three groups on the basis of preference profiles in choice games (social value orientation: prosocial, individualistic, or competitive). Attachment and sibling composition are measured via questionnaire or interview. Proportion in each orientation group is computed by age cohort. Logistic regression models the log-odds of group membership as a function of attachment, sibling count, and age. Temporal trends in group prevalence are estimated.

Regulatory task (emotional suppression or thought control) is performed. Subsequent performance on a self-control-demanding task (physical stamina, anagram persistence, emotion expression control) is measured. Paired or unpaired comparisons via t-test or within-subjects ANOVA quantify the performance decrement. The mechanism is interpreted as resource depletion: prior self-regulatory exertion reduces capacity for subsequent control.

Entities are presented with stimuli (figures or objects) and select the most preferred target. Stimuli vary in their deviation from a statistical modal option (uniqueness). Selection proportions for unique versus conformist options are computed. Chi-square tests or logit models compare selection patterns between cultural groups. In Study 4, ad text and imagery are coded for uniqueness/conformity themes, and frequency differences between cultures are tested.

Individuals are assessed on two continuous dimensions (Environmental Mastery and Personal Growth) via questionnaire. Cluster analysis or threshold-based classification assigns each entity to one of three groups (Achievers, Conservers, Seekers). Four maturity criteria (competence, generativity, ego development, wisdom) are measured for each group at two time points. Personality at baseline is used as a predictor. Mediation analysis tests whether identity at age 43 mediates the effect of age-21 personality on age-60 mental health pattern.

Participants rate emotions of shame or guilt in response to hypothetical scenarios (or recall own experiences) that vary in public-exposure and transgression/competence dimensions. Proportion or intensity of shame attribution is compared between high-exposure and low-exposure conditions using chi-square, Mann-Whitney U, or logit models. Coding of literary passages provides a replication via content-analysis frequency counts.

Entities complete implicit-association tasks (IAT) measuring math-male association strength and self-female association. Explicit attitudes toward math versus arts are rated. Group membership (gender) and self-identification are recorded. Correlation analysis and regression quantify the association between stereotype strength and attitudes, separately by gender. Path analysis may model chains of association (math=male -> self-female -> math-self incongruence -> negative attitudes).
domain: social psychology and gender stereotype effects

mechanism: Children's personality is assessed at baseline (ages 8-12) via questionnaire or observer ratings. A decade later, personality is reassessed in adulthood (ages 17-23), and concurrent behavioral outcomes (social/romantic success, antisocial conduct, maladaptation) are measured. Longitudinal regression models predict adult outcomes from childhood personality and adult personality. Correlation analysis quantifies the persistence of personality dimensions across time.

An actor performs a constrained behavior in a scenario. Observers rate the actor's attitude (inferred trait) that corresponds to the behavior. Diagnosticity of the behavior (how informative it is about true attitude) is manipulated. Between-subjects ANOVA or regression contrasts the correspondence bias (behavior-attitude correlation strength) across cultures and diagnosticity conditions. Mediation analysis estimates whether online inferences (generated during judgment) explain the cultural difference.
domain: cultural social psychology and attribution bias

mechanism: Entities complete Big Five personality inventories (multiple instruments). Criterion variables (behavioral outcomes: substance use, academic performance, etc.) are measured. Linear regression models criterion variables as functions of Big Five factor scores. Multiple prediction models are estimated across independent samples and across different personality instruments. Cross-sample consistency in regression coefficients is assessed via replication analysis.
